item.title: I'll let you in on a secret I learned from working in elder finance. — 15 replies — 56.7/hr
item.content:encoded:
I'll let you in on a secret I learned from working in elder finance.

Open thread • Replies: 15 • Updated: 2026-07-20 10:05 UTC

OP

I'll let you in on a secret I learned from working in elder finance. Literally every old person gets scammed out of their money to some extent, either by exorbitant nursing homes, selfish relatives, or just outright scams and frivolous lawsuits. Unless you have kids to protect you, your money WILL be taken from you once you're old. The world sees old people as resources to be harvested.

Early replies (first 3)

62500373
>>62500368
my bipolar elderly roastie (divorced, kids never visited her, alcoholic, didn't take her meds, etc.) was getting scammed by like 3 phone bills at once.

it had a happy ending when i called the police on her after a bipolar episode, and her brother (who she insisted was going to killer her) moved her in to some sort of assisted living facility.

62500375
>>62500373
elderly roastie NEIGHBOR

62500442
>>62500368
I'm not a fucking retard living in 1990 like my parents, I keep on top of trends and technology.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62500619
>>62500368
Everyone is getting scammed to some extent. What do you think inflation is doing to you? Government, banks, and their cronies stealing your money right out from under your nose.

62500684
>>62500368
Yea, they are the fat aphids in an ant nest waiting to be drained of their last drop of sap before their bodies are consumed when they get too old to eat.

62500950
>>62500368
>The world sees old people as resources to be harvested.

62501265
>>62500368

62501281

62501282
>>62500373

thought it was your mom lol

I've thought about doing another 4 years in college to become a geriatric doctor harvesting 90yo boomers for cash. That's 30 years of market right there.

62501402
>>62500950
You can be a elderly caregiver. My friend works as one, and it's drain all your energy.

62501473
So wr should booby trap the money is what youe saying.

62501552
>>62500368
> gets scammed out of their money
In this clown world, everything is outright scam.
Under 12 Years Old:

Fake online contests and prizes
Phishing through games and apps
Exploitation via social engineering

13–19 Years Old:

Fake job or internship offers
Romance and social media scams
Fake tickets, counterfeit products

20–29 Years Old:

Cryptocurrency and investment frauds
Online dating scams
Fake rental listings and scams

30–39 Years Old:

Loan and mortgage frauds
Business opportunity scams
Phishing for personal and financial info

40–49 Years Old:

Debt relief and financial scams
Fake charity appeals
Tech support and malware scams

50–59 Years Old:

Medicare and health insurance scams
Romance scams targeting loneliness
Investment and pyramid schemes

60+ Years Old:

Fake lotteries and sweepstakes
Impersonation scams (grandchildren, officials)
Health and charity frauds

62501561
>>62500368
yeah I'll probably set up some sort of elder care service or home to make some money on the side when I early retire, it's free money

62501587
>>62500368
good fuck boomerkikes for ruining the world

62501671
>>62500368
Even without full-on dementia, a lot of senior's brains just don't work as well.
For example, the brain shrinks with age.
Most also have some degree of cerebrovascular microcalcification.
My dad doesn't have dementia, but cognitively, he's just not able to handle bills, taxes, etc., though his paranoia helps him to avoid scams.
Mom also cognitively isn't where she used to be.
>>62501402
I'm a full-time caregiver for my parents (especially for my dad), and I wouldn't be surprised if this makes you more susceptible to scams.
The constant sleep deprivation and stress really takes a toll on you.
Like, I can feel my brain not working as well as it did even just a year or two ago.

Thoughts?

62498782

>>62498622
Wrong. Here’s an answer from AI (I asked for it to be understandable for someone with a 75 IQ, like you):
Prompt: Is it possible to receive backpay when granted Social Security Disability Insurance (SSDI)?

Answer: Yes. When Social Security approves SSDI, it may owe you money for earlier months when you qualified but had not yet been paid. This money is called backpay.

Usually:

* Social Security does not pay for the first five full months after your disability started.
* Payments can begin with the sixth full month.
* SSDI may also cover up to 12 months before you applied, depending on when your disability began.

Example: Social Security decides that your payable benefits should have started 20 months ago. It may give you those 20 missed monthly payments as one backpay payment, minus any lawyer’s fee or other required deductions.

62498810
>>62498782
No you're dumb.

62498858
>>62498782

62498860
>>62498458

>>62498858
Here’s an AI response for you:
It happens more often than people realize. If someone’s claim takes years because of appeals, Social Security can award benefits retroactively to the established onset date (subject to the statutory rules). The back pay is paid as a lump sum once the claim is finally approved. Five years is uncommon, but it’s absolutely possible in cases with long delays.
>>62498860
> ive been NEET for the last 6 years but have no income itd be nice to get something back from all the taxes i paid for the 2 decades of wagecucking i did
You’re running the risk of applying too far from the time you worked. Just read up on everything there is to know about SSDI (pay attention to Date Last Insured, in your case). Make sure you fill out the application perfectly. Have a few grand set aside for expert opinions. When SSA denies you, hire a good SSDI lawyer. And wait.

>>62497567
As you probably already know, most of the top tech companies are doing circular financing between themselves. This creates earnings (E) out of thin air and suppresses PE ratios. No guarantee they can keep doing this into the future.
Just one example:
Google's Q1 net income pumped 63%(!!) YoY. But half of their net income gain was due to "income from non-marketable equity securities". Basically, their stock holdings in OpenAI and Anthropic appreciated. Without this appreciation their net income would have not increased 63% YoY but instead been negative. And if/once OpenAI/Anthropic valuations drop, then Google will start to post losses instead.

They know people are looking at PE ratio now. Thus they adapt and start manipulating the ratio. This creates complacency in normie stock investors and allows for a bubble to happen. Next bubble they will have adapted in some different way again.
>But it's only tech stocks that are doing this circular financing, not the whole SP500
Mostly, yeah, but tech is now something like probably over half of SP500

I too remain skeptical until I see actual usage, but these partnerships do mean something.

>Why not just use the existing financial system which handles a billion transactions a day and has been in constant use and improvement for like 60 years?
You are looking at one of those improvements anon. Anything that can shave time off of transactions, reduce overhead, reduce complexity or risk will be considered. If it's shit, it will be discarded. Fedwire had been successfully handling transactions telegraphically for 50 years in the 1970s, yet at some point they trialed and switched to Telex and then to computers.

The fact that blockchain tech (in combination with oracles) has survived multiple rounds of trials and demos, with more and more financial institutions setting up teams to build out this stuff, means there is meat to it. How big that change/disruption is remains to be seen, and even then it's not guaranteed you will be able to position yourself in a way that allows you to profit from it. I think Chainlink has a pretty good chance of being that ticket, but we'll see.

item.title: Why is there so much negativity surrounding the UK? — 119 replies — 11.4/hr
item.content:encoded:
Why is there so much negativity surrounding the UK?

Open thread • Replies: 119 • Updated: 2026-07-19 23:52 UTC

OP

Every time I see the UK mentioned here, it is always in a negative light.

It is always
>In the USA, wages are better!
>In Europe, houses are cheaper!
>The FTSE is underperforming!
>My taxes are too high!

There is never any mention of the fantastic benefits which come with life in the UK. The fantastic healthcare for minimal cost, the great education system available to everybody, the dynamic modern economy with many bustling sectors, and everything else.

Taxes are higher than the USA but they are lower than most of Europe and we still enjoy fantastic public services whereas in America there are no public services or safety nets.

Not to mention the things you cannot measure. Being able to walk the streets in safety without fear of gun violence and gangs. Enjoying the fantastic beautiful nature and gardens. Living in the historical majesty and balancing that with modern cosmopolitan qualities.

The UK might not be the best place to become rich but nobody is poor here and it is very easy to enjoy life if you have goals beyond the number in your bank account.

Early replies (first 3)

>>62481958
the problem is that its being filled with immigrants who are living off tax payers money
thats the gist of why everyone is so pissed off here

No, I think it runs deeper than that. Most people have not personally had negative experiences with migrants, and reading the Daily Mail does not count.

I am trying to angle from a financial perspective too. People talk about the UK like it is a poor country but it is one of the richest countries on the planet. I think perhaps they are just spoiled and do not know what poor countries are really like. I blame social media.

I want to personally thank every Everest ID hater in here, talking to you Link baggies. They secured MiCA CASP last week.

A single MFSA license is valid across 27 EU states, reaching ~450M people. Of the ~283 CASPs on the ESMA register, nearly all are exchanges or brokers. Everest is one of the rare licensed infrastructure providers: identity and payment rails, not another venue.

Enjoy it furries

item.title: As I was saying yesterday. The Chinese are slowly giving the news to trading partners. No more dollars. You want to buy… — 77 replies — 4.6/hr

As I was saying yesterday. The Chinese are slowly giving the news to trading partners. No more dollars. You want to buy…

Open thread • Replies: 77 • Updated: 2026-07-19 17:47 UTC

OP

As I was saying yesterday. The Chinese are slowly giving the news to trading partners. No more dollars. You want to buy from us. You get a Yuan swap line. You settle trade imbalances in gold or Yuan and use our settlement systems.

What can the US do? All they have is threats against the likes of Argentina. China has the vital goods they need at the right prices. The will continue to do this and kill dollar dominance.

Theres no other choice or enter an inflationary spiral.

Early replies (first 3)

They publicly SAID they are going for it. Meanwhile, they are stacking gold in unknown but very large amounts, streamlining gold import/export regs, opening exchanges, and curtailing paper gold trading. Does that all seem like random events to you? Doesn't to me.

62498284
>>62497162
How much value has the dollar lost in those twenty years?

62498292
>>62497725
It soaks up currency so the government/banks can create and spend massive quantities of new currency with minimal inflation.

62498381
>>62498282
The Chinese operate on an extremely long time scale. “Going for it” could be “In fifty years we aim to be the world reserve currency.” I have no doubt they’re going for it; they’re going for everything. The fundamental problem they face is that they are still an export economy and are not yet equipped to be the global financial hub (hell, there is barely an alternative to SWIFT and even then, its only used in a few trades between countries). They still manage their currency and artificially inflate/deflate the yuan as they see fit. I understand the argument that they want to be gold-backed, but that’s a long ways away. You’re jumping the gun.

62498391
>>62498292
yea so that non-whites can get welfare and boomers can get health care

62498444
>>62498391
The non-meme answer to your question is that much of America’s modern history is inseparable from its identity as the world reserve currency. The property, infrastructure, quality of life, etc. that you may feel are decaying are decaying *relative* to how good things were, and “how good things were” is the result of being the world reserve currency. So you may look around now and say you don’t see the benefits, but the reality is that all of contemporary American life is in the shadow of a litany of benefits, decades and decades of benefits.

62498492
>>62498381
To evaluate China's strategy, you must take into account how badly the USA is managing its financial system. Really all China needs to do is sit back and wait while the US burns down its own house. All roads lead to massive inflation of the USD. At some point the world is going to start feeling the pain and then China, massive exporter that it is, will only need to say "10% discount for paying in our new gold-backed yuan." Then 20%. Then 50%. Then "We no longer accept USD." It might take a few years, but the USA is incapable of stopping it. No shooting, no shouting, just economic power, and the fruit of decades of American greed and corruption.

It WILL happen. You say "long ways away". Maybe. I say the interest on the US national debt will consume ALL Federal revenue in less than ten years. And that will be the turning point (if we make it that far). The bridge is out and there is zero political will to stop the train. And when the USD crashes, China will be ready to pick up the pieces.

62498507
>>62498391
You are seeing the end days. From WWII until the early 80s, America had an economic boom like the world had never seen and it was a flood that raised all ships. But it was unsustainable. It gave us the k-shaped economy. And now it is breaking.

China saw that prosperity and wants it. Keep in mind that the CCP has made some colossal blunders with its central planning. And it will likely eventually bungle a gold-backed currency. But before that happens, gold stackers will do very nicely thank you.

62498511
>>62498444
This is correct.

This is where you’re definitely wrong. There will be shooting. If you think the US will politely surrender its financial position and quietly slip into economic collapse, you are mistaken.

You don't even have to speculate that current holders of treasuries will shy away as the end approaches or that Federal revenue is going to drop as the recession continues. MMT is in its death spiral.
